title: #eng: 1778881234
preview:
Ava Chen: Quick idea — try a condensed 30m triage window for next two weeks to clear our >48h backlog. Proposal: 10m hotfix lane, 15m quick PR unblock, 5m owner assignments. Thoughts? :thinking_face:

Jamal Rivera: Seems doable. Can we shift from 11:00->11:30 to 10:30->11:00 to catch EU overlap?

Priya Nair: I prefer 10:30 start. Also can we add a recurring 'owner check' item to ensure tickets don't get stuck for 3+ days?

Luca Bianchi: +1 on owner check. Suggest we rotate the 'lead' each day — lead does the quick assignment and follow-ups.

Ava Chen: ok. Draft agenda:
```
10:30-10:40 hotfix lane (triage-bot flags)
10:40-10:55 PR/bug quick triage (assign owners)
10:55-11:00 owner rollcall + blockers
```

Marta Gómez: small ask — can we surface flaky tests and infra alerts separately? Maybe add a pinned thread in #eng-infra for infra FYIs.

Jamal Rivera: I'll take lead Mon/Wed this week. :thumbsup:

triage-bot: Reminder created — Mon/Wed 10:30-11:00 in #eng. Calendar invite sent to participants.

Priya Nair: Ack. I've updated the sprint board, added a 'triage-ready' label for tickets to surface in the agenda.

Luca Bianchi: One more — can we try a 2-week pilot then revisit metrics: avg time in backlog, #ownerless tickets, closed per session?

Ava Chen: Yep — we'll measure: avg time >48h, tickets closed per session, owner assignment rate. If no objections by EOD I'll update the cadence and ping ops to adjust calendar invites.

(Reaction notes: several :thumbsup: and a few :eyes: on the agenda.)

title: #eng: 1768625500
preview:
Dylan Brooks: heads up — we found duplicate event emission on `signup_completed` + `activation_first_request` (looks like it fires twice on some redirect paths). this is skewing the activation funnel + email trigger cohorts. quick patch PR is up, can we get fast reviews?

Dylan Brooks: PR: https://github.com/redwood/redwood/pull/28521 ("Fix duplicate signup event emission")

Dylan Brooks: repro is basically: sign up -> verify email -> back button -> continue -> we emit twice. also seeing it from retry logic on the client.

Dylan Brooks: logs from prod sample (same `event_id` missing, so both pass through):
```
[events] name=signup_completed user_id=usr_8c1... session_id=sess_33a... request_id=req_01HT... ts=...
[events] name=signup_completed user_id=usr_8c1... session_id=sess_33a... request_id=req_01HT... ts=... (+120ms)
```

Nadia Rahman: :eyes: taking a look. do we have an idempotency key now or just dedupe on server by (user_id, name, window)?

Dylan Brooks: server-side dedupe in the ingestion layer. PR adds a `client_event_uuid` + also a short window dedupe for legacy clients. (we can’t rely on request_id bc it can change)

Logan Wright: +1 we need this asap — dashboard is showing weird >100% conversions in a couple cohorts. what’s the dedupe window?

Dylan Brooks: 10s window keyed on (user_id, event_name, client_event_uuid if present else session_id + path). only for the handful of onboarding events.

Ethan Park: reviewing now. does this affect downstream billing/metering or just lifecycle analytics?

Dylan Brooks: just product analytics + lifecycle triggers. metering uses token usage events so no direct $ impact, but it does affect “first_success” segments.

Mei Lin: data side: we also saw this in the warehouse — duplicates started around the console redirect change last week. if this lands today, we should annotate the funnel dataset for the spike window.

Allison Grant: +1 on annotation. also please confirm we’re not dropping legitimate retries (e.g., offline -> resend) with the 10s window.

Dylan Brooks: yep, legitimate retries should have different `client_event_uuid` once SDK update is in. for legacy, 10s is conservative; the duplicates we see are within <1s.

Jada Williams: quick question: how does this interact with experiment cohort assignment? if cohort is set on signup event and we dedupe, are we stable?

Dylan Brooks: cohort assignment happens earlier (server-side at account create). these events are just emitting + used for funnel stages and email triggers.

Benji Okafor: can we ship this behind a flag in ingestion? in case we need to roll back quickly.

Dylan Brooks: PR includes a kill switch: `EVENT_DEDUPE_ONBOARDING_ENABLED` default on for 10% then ramp.

DeployBot: Buildkite build #118402 for PR #28521 started. (<https://buildkite.com/redwood/redwood/builds/118402|view>)

Nadia Rahman: left 2 minor comments (naming + add `source=console` to dedupe key for one event). otherwise LGTM.

Ethan Park: approved. only nit: can we log when we dedupe (count metric) so we can validate drop post-deploy?

Dylan Brooks: good call — added `events.deduped.count` metric + tags (event_name, source). pushed.

DeployBot: Buildkite build #118402 passed. (<https://buildkite.com/redwood/redwood/builds/118402|view>)

Logan Wright: approved from analytics side. pls ping when deployed so we can re-run the sanity queries.

Dylan Brooks: merged. going to request hotfix deploy to prod console + ingestion today if release manager ok.

Benji Okafor: I can take the deploy. what’s the target window?

Dylan Brooks: next 1–2 hrs ideally (before the next email send batch). also FYI marketing asked for confirmation since triggers were noisy.

DeployBot: PR #28521 merged into main by Dylan Brooks.

Allison Grant: once it’s out, I’ll update the activation dashboard annotation + alert thresholds (the duplicates were tripping the “activation jump” alert).

Mei Lin: I’ll backfill a deduped view for the affected date range for the readout, but long-term should be fine once fixed.

Dylan Brooks: thanks all. Benji lmk when deploy is complete; I’ll ping Logan/Nadia for validation.

Benji Okafor: deployed to ingestion at 1768629042. ramped to 10% and watching dedupe metric now.

title: #eng: 1764200501
preview:
Talia Benson: hey folks — looking for reviewers this week for the main Optimize quality gating PR chain. trying to keep this moving so we can hit internal beta in Jan.

Talia Benson: dependency chain (roughly):
1) config diff schema + static validation (redwood)
2) eval-harness trigger endpoint + retry/flake aggregation (eval-harness)
3) optimize service wiring + gate state machine + enforcement on apply (redwood)
4) console/api surfacing + UI blocks + override flow (redwood)

Talia Benson: PRs (please grab what you can):
- redwood PR-19511 add config-diff schema + validation rules
- eval-harness PR-412 trigger endpoint for Optimize gating
- eval-harness PR-418 flake score + rerun policy
- redwood PR-19502 gate-run state machine + persistence
- redwood PR-19523 wire Optimize -> eval-harness, retries/timeouts/artifacts
- redwood PR-19531 block apply when gate fails + surface reasons
- redwood PR-19537 override flow (expiring) + audit events
- redwood PR-19570 add artifact links in Console API

Talia Benson: reviewers needed esp for: runtime safety (batching/cache knobs), eval rigor, and “enforcement lives in the right place” (defense in depth).

Aditya Rao: +1 — if you can only do one pass, focus on 19531 (apply boundary) and 19537 (overrides). those are the ones we’ll get hammered on in customer/security reviews.

Jordan Lee: I can take 19531 + 19502 (state machine) from policy/enforcement angle. any gotchas called out from architecture thread? want to make sure we’re not relying on Console-only enforcement.

Talia Benson: yep, requirement is defense-in-depth: Optimize API must block apply, rollout controller will also block promotion later. Console is just a UI surface.

Benji Okafor: I’ll review 19511. quick q: does schema include per-change-type validation or is it just structural? (e.g., disallow cache+batch setting combos we know are unsafe)

Noah Patel: we added a minimal set of static checks in the PR description but likely need more. I’ll leave comments w/ a “safe-by-default” list vs “requires stricter gates”.

Mateo Alvarez: I can review 19511 + 19523 for runtime mapping. I want to confirm config diff -> runtime knobs is unambiguous and we’re logging the resolved effective config that eval ran against.

Olivia Grant: I’ll take eval-harness PR-412. main things I’m watching: input contract (baseline selection IDs, prompt set IDs), artifact pointers, and making sure we have a stable “result schema” for Console.

Jonas Weber: I can review PR-418 for flake scoring / reruns. please tag me if there’s any “pass on first run” logic — we need min sample sizes + aggregation rules to avoid false confidence.

Sahana Iyer: I can take a look at 19523 for timeouts/retry behavior. we should align w/ CI plan + GPU scarcity. do we have backoff caps per tier (smoke vs full)?

Connor O'Brien: +1, also want to ensure we don’t block merge queues forever. please include a clear “what fails fast vs what is async” note in the PRs.

Dylan Brooks: I’ll review 19570 (Console API) from tooling side. want to confirm we’re returning a compact summary + stable artifact links, not huge payloads.

Chloe Martin: I can pair w/ Dylan on 19570. also want to sanity check failure reasons are user-readable and map to UI copy (blocked, override requested, override granted/expired).

Kenji Nakamura: if there are UI strings already in 19531/19570, please @ me. I’m trying to keep wording consistent: “Quality gate failed” vs “insufficient confidence” vs “eval suite flaky/quarantined”.

Allison Grant: I’ll skim 19523/19531 for logging/metrics hooks. if we’re returning failure reasons, we should emit the same structured reason codes for dashboards/alerts.

Sergio Costa: +1 — especially distinguishing “real regression” vs “eval infra timeout” vs “validation rejected”. otherwise alerts will be noisy.

Marissa Cole: can we add a short checklist in the root PR descriptions? 
- gate run persisted
- artifacts link recorded
- apply boundary enforcement
- override + audit
- console surface
Helps product sign-off.

Elliot Price: once we’re close, I can start pulling notes for docs. if the override flow is changing (approvers/expiration), please keep the PR description updated so external docs don’t drift.

Talia Benson: amazing, thanks. I’ll drop assignments + due dates in Linear and link back here. also: if you see missing tests, please call it out early — we’ve got an E2E test PR planned but want to avoid whack-a-mole late.

Jordan Lee: claiming 19531 now. will leave comments today.

Benji Okafor: claimed 19511. will focus on schema stability + validation reason codes.

Olivia Grant: starting on 412. if anyone has opinions on baseline identifier format (route/model/hw sku), send them my way.

Jonas Weber: on 418. will pay attention to aggregation + how we store per-run variance.

Talia Benson: :thumbsup: thanks all — tagging owners directly on each PR now.

title: #eng: 1759942015
preview:
Jae Kim: quick q on the status page component mapping work — for route/capability tagging, what’s the expected labels/values? i’m looking at gateway metrics + a couple workers and want to make sure we tag in the “approved” way so it actually maps to public components.

Logan Wright: yup, we standardized on 3 dims for anything that can drive status/public summaries:
- `component_id`
- `region`
- `capability`
This is the same set incident-bot is collecting in intake now.

Jae Kim: ok but what counts as “capability” vs “route”? e.g. `/v1/chat/completions` + stream=true is that “chat” or “streaming”? + do we include model family anywhere?

Mina Haddad: capability is intentionally coarse + user-visible. don’t put model names in tags.
Rules of thumb:
- `/v1/chat/completions` (non-stream) => `capability=chat`
- `/v1/chat/completions` with `stream=true` OR SSE endpoint => `capability=streaming`
- `/v1/embeddings` => `capability=embeddings`
- `/v1/rerank` => `capability=reranking`
If something is “internal route-ish” (scheduler calls, prefill service), keep that as internal labels but still map outward to a capability.

Christine Wong: +1. also: `component_id` is product surface, not service. for almost all request-path metrics in prod it should be `component_id=hosted_api` (Dedicated/Private are different; we can go over those separately).

Allison Grant: docs pointer: Confluence has the taxonomy + definitions: “Status page component taxonomy” (SRE → status-page-and-comms). the canonical ids are lowercase snake_case. pls don’t invent new ones ad-hoc.

Jae Kim: do we have a schema somewhere i can validate against? i keep fat-fingering region names.

Logan Wright: yep, schema is in the registry validation PR. the “single source of truth” is a JSON registry file (and a JSON schema that CI validates). you can search in the observability-pack repo for “status component registry schema”.

Caleb Johnson: regions should match what routing uses today (no cloud provider AZs). examples we’re using:
- `us-east`
- `us-west`
- `eu-west`
- `ap-southeast`
If you only know “global” (rare), we’d rather you plumb region through than tag `global`.

Devon Price: for incident-bot intake fields we also use those exact strings. so if you tag metrics with `region=us-east` and pick `capability=streaming`, the bot drafts will line up with the component options.

Jae Kim: can someone drop an example tag block for gateway?

Christine Wong: from the gateway side (pseudo):
```python
metrics.increment(
  "gateway.request",
  tags={
    "component_id": "hosted_api",
    "region": request.region,  # e.g. "us-east"
    "capability": capability_from_request(request),
  },
)
```
`capability_from_request` should be based on endpoint + stream flag. not on model.

Mina Haddad: and if you’re doing this in traces too, same keys. we want to be able to pivot dashboards + summaries by the same dims.

Allison Grant: re: “route” — if you really need it for debugging, keep it as `route_name` or `endpoint` internally, but do not use it as a public-facing grouping. we had the “API ok but embeddings failing” issue because things were tagged as generic API without the capability split.

Jae Kim: makes sense. what about dedicated control plane? i’m touching a metric in the capacity scheduler.

Rafael Mendes: dedicated control plane is a separate component_id (not `hosted_api`). the taxonomy doc calls it out as “Dedicated: Control Plane” and we’re exposing a limited set of signals publicly. if your metric affects customer-facing provisioning/scale actions, tag it to that. if it’s purely internal noise, don’t.

Devon Price: + note: dedicated/private incidents have comms constraints. don’t include customer identifiers in tags/labels. component_id + region + capability is safe.

Elliot Price: if you need “what do we say publicly” guidance, docs side has a short section under incident templates. but for tagging, stick to the schema + examples above.

Jae Kim: awesome. i’ll implement with `capability=streaming` on chat stream path + keep `route_name` internal. will ping if CI yells at me.

Christine Wong: :thumbsup: if CI fails, it’ll usually point at invalid enum value. easiest fix is copy/paste from the registry.

Logan Wright: also worth checking the hotfix PR we landed last week (misclassified embeddings vs chat). the failure mode was exactly “wrong capability tag → wrong component update”. so thank you for being careful here 🙏

Rafael Mendes: dedicated control plane is a separate component_id (not `hosted_api`). the taxonomy doc calls it out as “Dedicated: Control Plane” and we’re exposing a limited set of signals publicly. if your metric affects customer-facing provisioning/scale actions, tag it to that. if it’s purely internal noise, don’t.

Devon Price: + note: dedicated/private incidents have comms constraints. don’t include customer identifiers in tags/labels. component_id + region + capability is safe.

Elliot Price: if you need “what do we say publicly” guidance, docs side has a short section under incident templates. but for tagging, stick to the schema + examples above.

Jae Kim: awesome. i’ll implement with `capability=streaming` on chat stream path + keep `route_name` internal. will ping if CI yells at me.

Christine Wong: :thumbsup: if CI fails, it’ll usually point at invalid enum value. easiest fix is copy/paste from the registry.

Logan Wright: also worth checking the hotfix PR we landed last week (misclassified embeddings vs chat). the failure mode was exactly “wrong capability tag → wrong component update”. so thank you for being careful here 🙏

title: #eng: 1747333303
preview:
Priya Natarajan: We’re close to flipping the oncall handoff for the PLG signal router. Can folks drop the *must-have* metrics + alert conditions? Thinking HubSpot 429s, retries, dropped events, lag, etc. Also owners + dashboard link placeholders so we can wire this into PagerDuty.

Logan Wright: +1. From telemetry/analytics side, the top things that will bite us:
- event lag (telemetry -> analytics table -> scoring output)
- aggregator job failures / partial writes
- dropped events / DLQ growth
If we don’t alert on lag, sales will just see “nothing happening” and assume routing is broken.

Mei Lin: For the daily aggregation job (ENG-7732 / PR-28458), we should alert on:
- `aggregation_job_success` (1/0) per run
- `aggregation_rows_written` (unexpectedly low vs trailing 7d baseline)
- `aggregation_backfill_runs` (if backfill is running, suppress some lag alerts)
Also a gauge for `analytics_table_partition_freshness_hours`.

Caleb Johnson: Ingestion/data quality:
- `telemetry_event_ingest_lag_seconds` p95 and max
- `telemetry_events_dropped_total` (with reason label: schema_invalid, auth, throttled)
- `telemetry_events_dlq_depth`
We should page only on sustained DLQ growth + lag, not on occasional schema_invalid spikes (docs instrumentation can be noisy).

Ethan Park: HubSpot integration metrics (PR-28503 + PR-28544):
- `hubspot_requests_total` by endpoint
- `hubspot_429s_total` + `hubspot_rate_limited_seconds_total`
- `hubspot_5xx_total`
- `hubspot_retries_total` + `hubspot_retry_queue_depth`
- `hubspot_task_create_failures_total`
- `hubspot_company_upsert_failures_total`
Also *idempotency* issues are subtle — a counter for `hubspot_deduped_updates_total` would help validate we’re not spamming timeline events.

Nadia Rahman: Scoring service should expose:
- `signals_created_total` (by signal_type)
- `signals_suppressed_total` (by suppression_reason)
- `signals_routed_total` (by destination=hubspot)
- `signals_failed_total` (validation_failed, missing_company_mapping, hubspot_error)
And a histogram: `signal_end_to_end_latency_seconds` (telemetry timestamp -> task created)

Marcus Lin: From security side: please add a metric + alert if we ever attempt to send disallowed fields to HubSpot.
Like `hubspot_payload_policy_violations_total` (should be 0 always). If >0, page the oncall + auto-disable routing.

Talia Benson: Capturing this for the runbook. Can we agree on *alert thresholds* (page vs ticket)? Proposed:
P0 page:
- HubSpot 429s sustained (>= 10% of requests for 10m) OR retry queue depth continuously increasing for 15m
- End-to-end lag p95 > 6h (excluding backfills)
- Task create failures > 2% for 10m
P1 ticket:
- 5xx spikes but recovering
- row writes drop >50% vs 7d median
Thoughts?

Logan Wright: 6h lag feels high given we’re doing daily windows, but the expectation from sales is “I should see tasks same day.” I’d set:
- ticket at p95 > 2h for 2 consecutive hours
- page at p95 > 6h for 2 consecutive hours
and explicitly suppress during backfill.

Mei Lin: +1 to suppression during backfills. We can tag runs with `window_type=daily|backfill` and key alerts off daily only.
Also add:
- `aggregation_window_complete_timestamp` gauge (epoch) so we can compute freshness in Grafana easily.

Ethan Park: On 429s: HubSpot rate limits are bursty. I’d rather page on “routing stopped” symptoms:
- `hubspot_task_create_success_rate` < 95% for 15m (page)
- `hubspot_retry_queue_depth` > 5k and increasing (page)
And a warning when 429 ratio > 5% for 10m (ticket) so we tune batching.

Caleb Johnson: For dropped events: page if `telemetry_events_dropped_total{reason!=schema_invalid}` increases by > 1k in 10m AND lag p95 > 1h. Otherwise we’ll page every time a client SDK rolls.

Priya Natarajan: This is great. Let’s lock a minimal set for day-1 oncall:
1) HubSpot delivery health (success rate, retries, 429/5xx)
2) E2E lag
3) DLQ depth / dropped events
4) Job/run success
Anything beyond that can be phase 2.

Nadia Rahman: Please also include a “volume sanity” panel:
- `signals_created_total` by type
- `signals_routed_total` by region
If we suddenly double our routing volume, that’s usually a threshold regression.

Talia Benson: Owners?
- HubSpot metrics/alerts: @Ethan Park
- Ingestion lag/DLQ/drops: @Caleb Johnson
- Aggregation freshness + writes: @Mei Lin
- Overall dashboards + PD integration: I can coordinate but need a DRI for the service

Priya Natarajan: DRI for service ops is Logan (Telemetry & Analytics) for this pilot. Ethan owns HubSpot path. I’ll be escalation for platform.

DeployBot: reminder: PR-28630 (metrics hooks) merged to main. Staging deploy scheduled 18:00 PT.

Logan Wright: Dashboard placeholders (will fill once Grafana panels are live):
- Signal Router Overview: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/PLG_SIGNAL_ROUTER/overview (TBD)
- HubSpot Delivery: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/PLG_SIGNAL_ROUTER/hubspot (TBD)
- Ingestion + DLQ: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/PLG_SIGNAL_ROUTER/ingestion (TBD)
Runbook placeholder: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENG/PLG+Signal+Router+Oncall+Runbook (TBD)

Marcus Lin: +1 on runbook including “kill switch” steps if payload policy violations fire.

Priya Natarajan: Action items:
- Ethan: define alert rules for HubSpot success rate + retry queue depth (and add to PD service)
- Mei/Caleb: freshness + DLQ alerts with backfill suppression
- Talia: create runbook skeleton + link to INT-1843 + suppression guidance
Let’s aim to have alerts in place before we expand pilot cohort next week.

title: #eng: 1746816155
preview:
Connor O'Brien: Heads up: airgap bundle from the latest private installer build is missing the Grafana dashboards for the Private observability min pack. On-prem install comes up, Prometheus scrapes OK, but Grafana has *zero* Redwood dashboards.

Fatima Noor: Confirmed on my side. Using the onprem offline flow (no egress). Grafana is reachable, datasource is configured, but dashboard folder is empty. I only see default Grafana stuff.

Diego Santos: Ugh. Is it just dashboards or also alert rules?

Fatima Noor: Alerts *seem* present (I see the PrometheusRule objects). The missing bit looks like the dashboard JSONs aren’t in the airgap tarball at all.

Dylan Brooks: That matches what I saw in staging earlier this week. The Helm chart references dashboards via ConfigMaps, but the installer’s airgap bundle needs to include the `observability-pack` dashboards directory (or the generated ConfigMap manifests). Might be a manifest omission in the bundler.

Chloe Martin: Quick check: are we expecting the installer to ship raw dashboard JSONs and render ConfigMaps at install-time, or ship pre-rendered ConfigMaps? I thought we went with “ship raw dashboards + grafana sidecar loads them”.

Diego Santos: For Private installer we’re packaging the raw JSON artifacts + the helm bits. If those JSONs aren’t in the bundle, nothing to mount, so sidecar has nothing to load.

Connor O'Brien: I unpacked the artifact and searched:
`tar -tf redwood-private-airgap-bundle.tgz | grep -i grafana`
Nothing except the Grafana image tar.

Rafael Mendes: That explains the Support thread we saw last week (“dashboards blank after upgrade”). If this is the same root cause, we need a hotfix fast.

Sahana Iyer: CI didn’t catch this because smoke tests run in connected env and pull dashboards from the repo path. Airgap path uses the bundle manifest only. We should add an assertion that the bundle contains at least N dashboard JSON files.

Logan Wright: +1. Also can we verify the bundle includes the alert rules + recording rules too? Would rather not fix dashboards and then find rules missing.

Connor O'Brien: Root cause looks like `airgap-bundle-manifest.yaml` doesn’t include the dashboards/rules artifacts from `observability-pack` (only includes images + helm charts). Probably got dropped in the last refactor.

Dylan Brooks: I can take a look at the bundler config. It should include:
- `observability-pack/private-minimum/dashboards/*.json`
- `observability-pack/private-minimum/prometheus-rules/*.yaml`
And then installer should copy into the expected `/var/lib/redwood/observability/` path.

Chloe Martin: If we add raw dashboards, make sure we preserve folder structure so Grafana sidecar labels/folders still work (Runtime vs Control Plane vs Infra). Otherwise everything lands in “General”.

Diego Santos: Also ensure checksums update. Airgap customers verify SHA256 from the manifest.

Connor O'Brien: Proposed plan:
1) Patch installer bundle manifest to include dashboards + rules
2) Cut a hotfix PR + backport to release branch
3) Trigger airgap build + rerun onprem validation plan (at least: install, upgrade, confirm dashboards load)

Rafael Mendes: For severity: I’d call this a release-blocker for on-prem. Without dashboards the “minimum pack” story is broken.

Fatima Noor: Agree. For regulated customers, they specifically asked for turnkey dashboards in offline env. This is exactly the failure mode.

Sahana Iyer: I’ll add a CI step to the installer pipeline:
- unpack bundle
- `find . -name "*.json" | grep dashboards | wc -l` must be > 0
- and maybe validate presence of a known dashboard UID/title file

Elliot Price: Once fixed, can someone drop me the exact version + any user-facing notes? Docs currently say “dashboards included in offline bundle” so we should add a known-issue note for the broken versions + the fixed version.

Connor O'Brien: Dylan can you own the PR? I’ll coordinate the backport + release note.

Dylan Brooks: Yep. I’ll open a PR to `redwood-private-installer` updating the airgap manifest + post-install validation to fail loudly if dashboards directory is missing.

Diego Santos: Please add a preflight warning too. Something like: “observability artifacts not present in bundle; dashboards will be empty” so we don’t silently ship a broken pack.

Chloe Martin: Can we also add a quick postinstall check that Grafana has loaded at least one dashboard ConfigMap? Even a simple “list ConfigMaps with grafana_dashboard=1 label” would help.

Dylan Brooks: Good call. I’ll add a postinstall validation step:
`kubectl -n <ns> get cm -l grafana_dashboard=1 | grep redwood || exit 1`

Logan Wright: While you’re in there, make sure rules path is included too. We had a similar miss earlier where recording rules weren’t copied.

Dylan Brooks: Will do. I’ll verify both dashboard JSONs and rule YAMLs are bundled.

CI Bot: Build notification: nightly private-installer airgap job #482 succeeded (artifact uploaded).

Rafael Mendes: Nightly succeeded but doesn’t validate contents. Let’s not rely on that.

Connor O'Brien: Tracking: Dylan PR today, backport tomorrow AM, then Fatima to re-run the onprem airgap validation checklist.

Fatima Noor: I can rerun as soon as we have a new bundle. I’ll test:
- fresh airgap install
- upgrade from previous broken bundle -> fixed bundle
- confirm dashboards render and alerts present

Elliot Price: Please capture screenshots or at least the list of dashboards showing up. Helps for Support enablement.

Dylan Brooks: PR incoming in ~1 hour.

Connor O'Brien: Thanks all. Let’s keep this thread updated with the PR link + the fixed build ID once it’s cut.

title: #eng: 1739035050
preview:
Rafael Mendes: Heads up: scheduling a Private DR game day rehearsal next week. Need to lock scenario + roles today/tomorrow so we can prep runbooks + staging env. Two candidates: (A) region loss / restore into clean cluster, or (B) cluster corruption (control plane state / k8s etcd issues). Thoughts? Also need IC/comms/scribe + operators.

Talia Benson: :eyes: What day/time are you aiming for? We should pick something that doesn’t overlap w/ release cut.

Rafael Mendes: Tentative Wed 10:00–12:00 PT. Will confirm once we choose scenario.

Sean Gallagher: For usefulness, I’d vote B first. We’ve had more “restore drill failed because cluster is weird” than actual region loss. But we can simulate region loss by treating it as “new cluster” anyway.

Miles Thornton: +1 that “region loss” is mostly an infra story (DNS/TLS, new cluster bootstrap). “Cluster corruption” forces us to prove the operator workflow: identify blast radius, decide etcd restore vs app-level restore, verify tenancy/routing policies.

Ethan Park: If we do B, can we constrain it to control-plane datastore corruption (e.g., config DB / state) rather than full etcd? Full etcd restore gets hairy and may turn into infra-only exercise.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. If the objective is validating our backup artifact + installer `restore apply`, we should avoid etcd snapshot restore unless we *explicitly* want to test infra runbook.

Neha Kapoor: What success metric are we measuring? If it’s RTO for “Private control plane back online + can serve requests”, then we should simulate loss of control-plane state and force a deterministic restore. Otherwise we’ll spend 2h debating k8s.

Rafael Mendes: Goal is: prove Day-2 toolkit + runbooks for enterprise. So: consistent backup, restore into fresh cluster, validate.

Vivek Kulkarni: From customer angle, “region loss” wording resonates. But if we can implement it as “we lost the cluster/control plane, here’s how we restore elsewhere” that’s fine. In VPC docs we’re positioning a same-region clean cluster restore as baseline.

Colin O'Donnell: If we pick “region loss” in the narrative, we should include DNS/TLS steps in the checklist. Even in staging, we can fake it w/ internal DNS record + cert rotation note.

Kira Thompson: I can draft the comms + run-of-show, but I need crisp scope: are we expecting people to touch IAM/KMS setup during the drill? Or assume backup bucket + KMS are already provisioned.

Hanae Suzuki: Please assume KMS is provisioned. Otherwise the drill becomes “debug IAM” for 90 min. We can still include a check that restore fails safely if decrypt can’t happen.

Marcus Lin: +1. Also we should explicitly state we are NOT restoring secret material, just secret refs, so we don’t end up passing around decrypted payloads in Slack.

Connor O'Brien: For on-prem/air-gapped, we still need to validate signed bundle verification at least once, but that might be a separate game day. For this one (VPC), we can keep it simple.

Fatima Noor: If we’re skipping air-gapped this time, can we at least add one “offline artifact import” step as optional? Like: download artifact to local runner, re-upload, then restore. We’ve seen customer workflows where bucket access is segmented.

Sean Gallagher: That’s a good “inject friction” step without turning it into full air-gap.

Allison Grant: Observability angle: can we make sure the drill outputs a single “validation report” artifact (json/text) + emits metrics (duration, success/fail, error class)? I want something we can pin to the dashboard later.

title: #eng: 1738960013
preview:
Connor O'Brien: pulling folks into one thread re: feature flag + cohort mechanics for onboarding checklist MVP. We need to lock this this week for rollout plan + Support readiness. PR is up (pr-28467) but there are open questions.

Dylan Brooks: thx. current implementation in Console uses `onboarding_checklist_enabled` from config service. if false, we hide entrypoint + don’t call progress API. if true, UI loads and renders whatever state.

Chloe Martin: backend side (progress svc) is always on in prod (since gateway can emit events regardless). but we can gate the *Console access* + step details. I’m +1 to not gating event emission; otherwise we create data gaps.

Logan Wright: +1. telemetry wants events regardless so we can baseline funnel / TTFS pre/post. gating event emission by flag breaks comparability. we can add `flag_variant`/`exposure` as context in the events if we need.

Ben Carter: for cohorting, we should decide: tenant-level or user-level. Product asked for “accounts see checklist” not “some users in same org see different things”. I strongly recommend tenant-level.

Marissa Cole: tenant-level is the intent. we don’t want a support scenario where admin sees checklist and other teammate doesn’t.

Nikhil Sharma: agree. also for TTFS/TTFP reporting we care per tenant, so exposure should be stable.

Connor O'Brien: ok tenant-level. question: where do we compute cohort? console side vs server side.

Ethan Park: compute server-side in control plane/config. if Console computes cohort locally you can get drift (different SDK versions, different hashing). Better: config service returns `onboardingChecklist: { enabled: true, variant: "A", cohort: "dogfood" }`.

Dylan Brooks: today we’re calling `GET /v1/console/config` and it returns feature gates already. we can extend.

Talia Benson: also helps with “kill switch” - one place to flip.

Connor O'Brien: cool. what’s the cohort rule? what fields do we use for deterministic bucketing.

Ben Carter: do NOT use email domain. use `tenant_id` stable hash. segmentation rules layered on top:
1) allowlist internal dogfood tenants
2) allowlist a few design partners
3) then % rollout by tenant_id hash bucket
We can also exclude “enterprise” plan if we want, but we need clarity.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE perspective, % rollout must be a real kill switch (0%) and must be fast to propagate (<5 min). also: if there’s an outage in progress svc, the flag should fail closed (hide UI) not degrade console performance.

Chloe Martin: progress svc is behind the same edge as Console API. if it errors, UI can show “Checklist temporarily unavailable” and still render rest of Console. We can also have Console only call it if enabled.

Allison Grant: we should treat it as non-critical path. timeouts should be low (e.g., 300-500ms) + no retries from browser. avoid cascading issues.

Dylan Brooks: yup, I set 400ms timeout and on failure we drop the component (no blocking). we still log a metric + Sentry event.

Nadia Rahman: re: adding `flag_variant` to events: prefer server-side enrichment. gateway emits events; telemetry pipeline can join against exposure dataset keyed by tenant_id + timestamp. otherwise you leak flag evaluation details into multiple emitters.

Logan Wright: +1. we can write an “exposure snapshot” table in analytics daily + also a real-time KV for joins. but simplest: config svc also emits `onboarding.checklist.exposed` event when enabled flips from false->true for a tenant.

Connor O'Brien: do we need an explicit exposure event for counting? or can we just use config snapshot.

Logan Wright: for accurate funnel denominators, explicit exposure event is nice. but we can ship MVP with snapshot and add event later.

Marissa Cole: I’m ok with snapshot for MVP as long as we can answer “who saw the checklist” reliably. Support will ask.

Amara Diallo: question: if we don’t gate gateway event emission, do we risk “step complete” happening for tenants who never saw checklist? ex: api key created, first 2xx request, etc.

Chloe Martin: that’s fine. progress state is basically computed from behavior; showing UI is separate. if tenant later gets exposed, their checklist will appear partially complete (which might be good!).

Ben Carter: agree. and it reduces “cold start” confusion.

Nikhil Sharma: BUT: need to make sure step ordering doesn’t look weird if they already did prod stuff. e.g., “Set budget alerts” might be incomplete while “Send production traffic” looks complete.

Dylan Brooks: UX spec has “Recommended next” behavior. If some advanced steps are complete, we still show sequence but highlight the first incomplete. Also can collapse completed steps.

Talia Benson: ok. rollout mechanics: what cohorts for phase 1 dogfood?

Connor O'Brien: proposal:
- Week 1 internal (Redwood tenants) + 3 handpicked partner tenants
- Then 5% new self-serve signups (created_at >= rollout date)
- Then 25% all eligible
Need to decide if we only target *new* tenants or include existing.

Ben Carter: for activation lift we care mainly new. I’d do new-only initially to avoid surprising existing customers in Console. But telemetry wants some existing for baseline comparisons.

Logan Wright: baseline comparisons don’t require exposure; we can compare pre vs post on new signups only. I’m ok with new-only for the UI.

Marissa Cole: new-only simplifies Support training too (“If they signed up after X they probably have it”).

Sean Gallagher: new-only reduces blast radius. do it.

Chloe Martin: implementing “new-only” requires we have tenant.created_at in config service. do we already have that accessible?

Ethan Park: yes in control plane. config svc can evaluate rule `created_at >= 2025-02-xx`.

Dylan Brooks: one nuance: if we gate on created_at, tenants created just before date never get it unless we run a migration/override. maybe we want an allowlist for “requested access”.

Connor O'Brien: we can include an override list. Support asked for this anyway (ENG-5852 admin override is separate though). For rollout gating, we can maintain a “rollout allowlist” in config.

Ben Carter: yes. allowlist should be in control plane, editable by oncall/Release coord.

Allison Grant: please put guardrails: any manual allowlist change should be audited. doesn’t need full SOC2 today but at least who/when.

Ethan Park: we can log changes in config admin logs.

LaunchDarkly Bot: LaunchDarkly flag update: `console.onboarding_checklist` created in project `redwood-console`. Default: off. Rules: allowlist (internal tenants), then 0% rollout.

Dylan Brooks: wait we’re using LaunchDarkly directly? I thought we were keeping feature flags in control plane config.

Connor O'Brien: sorry, to clarify: pr-28467 uses LD SDK server-side in config service. Console reads config service, not LD directly.

Chloe Martin: ok that’s fine.

Nadia Rahman: if LD is used, please ensure config service caches evaluation results (per tenant) w/ TTL. don’t want every console page load hammering LD.

Ethan Park: caching is in place. We cache per tenant_id for 5 minutes; bust cache on config change webhook.

Sean Gallagher: 5 minutes ok. make sure kill switch propagation meets SRE ask. webhook path must be reliable.

Ethan Park: webhook already used for other flags. if webhook fails, worst case TTL 5 min.

Connor O'Brien: decision points to record:
1) tenant-level bucketing
2) config service evaluates flag (LD backed)
3) UI gated, event emission not gated
4) new tenants only initially + allowlist override
5) fail closed if progress svc down (hide component)
Anything else?

Marissa Cole: also: exclude enterprise contracts at first? I’d exclude anything with `plan_tier=enterprise` until Support + CS trained.

Ben Carter: I’d exclude enterprise by default, but allowlist for design partners.

Connor O'Brien: ok. Ethan can add rule in config service.

Dylan Brooks: I’ll update Console to display a small “Beta” pill only for dogfood cohort? or for all. UX spec says avoid “beta” unless we think it’s unstable.

Nikhil Sharma: avoid beta label; it makes customers nervous. use normal UI.

Talia Benson: timeline: can we land the rule changes today so we can start dogfood tomorrow?

Ethan Park: yes if decisions final. I’ll update config rules + add fields returned to Console: `enabled`, `cohort`, `rollout_reason` (for debugging; not shown in UI).

Allison Grant: careful with `rollout_reason` surfacing to client. don’t leak internal segmentation logic. keep it server logs only.

Dylan Brooks: +1. we can keep it behind an internal header or only in logs.

Connor O'Brien: ok. action items:
- Ethan: implement new-only + enterprise exclude + allowlist; keep reason server-side
- Dylan: ensure progress API calls are gated + timeout/fail closed + no retries
- Logan/Nadia: confirm telemetry approach for exposure denominator (snapshot ok MVP)
- Connor: update PM-768 + rollout doc with these decisions

Logan Wright: I’ll post in #eng-platform that telemetry is ok with snapshot for MVP, but we should add an exposure event in phase 2.

Sean Gallagher: and please define kill criteria. If error rate for progress svc > X or console p95 increases by Y, we flip to 0%.

Connor O'Brien: yep will add go/no-go + kill switch thresholds in rollout plan.

Marissa Cole: thanks all. once config is updated, ping me and I’ll sanity check in staging.

Dylan Brooks: will do. :thumbsup:

Connor O'Brien: decision points to record:
1) tenant-level bucketing
2) config service evaluates flag (LD backed)
3) UI gated, event emission not gated
4) new tenants only initially + allowlist override
5) fail closed if progress svc down (hide component)
Anything else?

Marissa Cole: also: exclude enterprise contracts at first? I’d exclude anything with `plan_tier=enterprise` until Support + CS trained.

Ben Carter: I’d exclude enterprise by default, but allowlist for design partners.

Connor O'Brien: ok. Ethan can add rule in config service.

Dylan Brooks: I’ll update Console to display a small “Beta” pill only for dogfood cohort? or for all. UX spec says avoid “beta” unless we think it’s unstable.

Nikhil Sharma: avoid beta label; it makes customers nervous. use normal UI.

Talia Benson: timeline: can we land the rule changes today so we can start dogfood tomorrow?

Ethan Park: yes if decisions final. I’ll update config rules + add fields returned to Console: `enabled`, `cohort`, `rollout_reason` (for debugging; not shown in UI).

Allison Grant: careful with `rollout_reason` surfacing to client. don’t leak internal segmentation logic. keep it server logs only.

Dylan Brooks: +1. we can keep it behind an internal header or only in logs.

Connor O'Brien: ok. action items:
- Ethan: implement new-only + enterprise exclude + allowlist; keep reason server-side
- Dylan: ensure progress API calls are gated + timeout/fail closed + no retries

Logan Wright: I’ll post in #eng-platform that telemetry is ok with snapshot for MVP, but we should add an exposure event in phase 2.

Sean Gallagher: and please define kill criteria. If error rate for progress svc > X or console p95 increases by Y, we flip to 0%.

Connor O'Brien: yep will add go/no-go + kill switch thresholds in rollout plan.

Dylan Brooks: will do. :thumbsup:

title: #eng: 1738691215
preview:
Vivek Kulkarni: Sharing early results from the customer requirement survey for Private backup/restore + DR. TL;DR the top constraints are exactly what we suspected: air-gap, NFS, and “KMS but not AWS KMS”.

Vivek Kulkarni: Link to sheet (internal): <https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/xyz|customer-requirements-backup-restore-survey>

Vivek Kulkarni: Summary (n=6 accounts; 3 regulated, 2 fintech, 1 SaaS w/ enterprise roadmap):
- Air-gapped/on-prem: 4/6 require *zero* outbound egress from the cluster. “Offline bundle + signed artifacts” called out explicitly.
- Backup storage backends allowed:
  - NFS/SMB preferred/required: 3/6 (one said “S3 is a hard no; internal filers only”)
  - S3 allowed: 2/6 but with customer-managed KMS keys and strict IAM
  - “Private cloud object store” (Ceph/MinIO): 2/6 (S3-compatible)
- KMS/HSM constraints:
  - 5/6 require CMK w/ rotation + auditability
  - 3/6 said “we have KMS but it’s not AWS/GCP” (Thales / internal HSM / Vault Transit)
  - 2/6 require dual control / break-glass procedure for key access
- RPO/RTO asks (self-reported):
  - RPO: 15m (2), 1h (2), 4h (2)
  - RTO: 2h (1), 4h (3), 24h (2)
- Operational expectations:
  - 4/6 want scheduled backups via k8s CronJob and a “last successful backup” signal they can alert on
  - 3/6 asked for restore validation / smoke test they can run quarterly for audit
  - “No secrets in backups” mentioned in 2 responses; they want references and a checklist for rehydration

Vivek Kulkarni: Open Qs called out by customers:
1) Does the backup include k8s resources/Helm values or only Redwood control-plane state?
2) How do we handle restore into a new cluster where CRDs/operators aren’t installed yet?
3) If KMS is down, does backup fail closed or fall back to local encryption key?

Talia Benson: super helpful. can you drop this into the spec draft too? also if you can tag which of these are “must have for purchase” vs “nice to have”, that’d help me with phase planning.

Markus Klein: +1. For FinBank specifically (from our last call), they basically said “air-gap + NFS + HSM-backed keys or we can’t proceed.” They also asked whether we can prove quarterly restore drills w/ a report artifact.

Fatima Noor: On the air-gap point: we should assume no access to public container registries, no pip/npm downloads, and no time sync guarantees. One customer note I saw: “install media delivered via SFTP/USB” (yes really). We’ll need deterministic bundle layout + checksum manifest + signature verify.

Hanae Suzuki: KMS constraint matches what I’ve been hearing. If 3/6 need non-cloud KMS, we should design the envelope encryption provider interface so it supports:
- AWS KMS (VPC)
- GCP KMS (if/when)
- Vault transit / HSM (on-prem)
Also we need a clear stance on what happens when KMS is unreachable (I’d vote fail-closed, but make it explicit).

Marcus Lin: Re: “no secrets in backups” — we should align with that. Backups should *not* contain raw secret material (tokens/keys) unless the customer explicitly opts in and understands the risk.
We can back up references + metadata + a “rehydrate secrets” step (KMS/HSM config, external secret store config). We’ll need to spell out threat model + evidence story for audits.

Sean Gallagher: If customers want alerting on last successful backup, we should standardize metrics now:
- backup_last_success_timestamp
- backup_duration_seconds
- backup_bytes_total
- backup_result (success/failure + reason)
Also restore validation cadence: at least monthly in staging for us; quarterly for customers feels realistic.

Bruno Silva: NFS being “required” for 3/6 is important. We should bake in guardrails:
- detect “insecure” mount options
- warn on noatime/actimeo weirdness
- performance expectations (NFS latency can kill snapshot times)
Maybe we provide a simple throughput test as part of pre-flight.

Neha Kapoor: On RPO 15m: we should be careful about what we can actually commit to. If control plane DB snapshot + object store copy takes longer than 15m in some envs, we need to either (a) set expectations per archetype or (b) recommend incremental / WAL shipping patterns if supported.

Kira Thompson: For docs/runbooks, can we codify this as a “Supported deployment archetypes + achievable RPO/RTO” matrix? Customers will otherwise assume best-case numbers apply everywhere.

Elliot Price: +1. Also from a docs standpoint, “air-gapped” needs its own section (offline bundle, import/export commands, verification steps, what to do if clocks are skewed). We should include a customer-facing FAQ like:
- what exactly gets backed up
- what is explicitly out of scope
- how to test restores
- what support can/can’t do

Vivek Kulkarni: Yep. I’ll update the spec draft with this and add a “purchase blocker” column where we have it. Immediate takeaways for scope:
- Must support NFS + S3-compatible object store (MinIO/Ceph) + S3
- Must support CMK and a path for non-cloud KMS (at least provider interface + “reference implementation”)
- Must ship signed offline bundle for on-prem/air-gapped
- Must provide metrics + a simple restore validation report

Vivek Kulkarni: For question (1) I’m leaning: backup = Redwood control-plane state + the minimal k8s manifests/Helm values needed for deterministic redeploy, but we should *not* pretend to be a generic cluster backup tool. We can provide Velero guidance separately.

Sean Gallagher: makes sense. If we keep it to “app-level” we can still document the “when to use etcd snapshot restore” escape hatch.

Marcus Lin: +1. As long as we clearly separate responsibilities, security review should be simpler.

Talia Benson: action items I’m hearing:
- Vivek: update spec w/ survey summary + blocker tags
- Hanae/Marcus: propose KMS provider interface + fail behavior (and confirm secrets stance)
- Bruno: NFS pre-flight checks proposal
- Elliot/Kira: outline customer-facing FAQ + archetype matrix

Vivek Kulkarni: ack. I’ll post an updated spec link later today.

title: #eng: 1738289001
preview:
Chloe Martin: Heads up: need eyes on the feature flag + RBAC changes for the internal Unit Economics dashboard before we merge. PR is up: https://github.com/redwood/redwood/pull/19397

Chloe Martin: Key question: initial allowlist. Who do we want in at launch vs after dogfood?

DeployBot: PR #19397 "feature flag + RBAC for internal unit economics dashboard" is waiting on review. Checks: lint ✅ unit ✅ policy-tests ✅

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Skimmed. Looks like you added `feature_flag: console.unit_econ_dashboard_internal` + RBAC permission `console:unit_econ:read` gated by group `unit-econ-dashboard-internal`.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Two things:
1) can we avoid any per-user allowlist in code? prefer group-based only.
2) confirm the API endpoints (time series + breakdowns) require BOTH flag and permission.

Felix Schneider: Yep endpoints check both. UI route is also behind the flag so folks don’t even see the nav item unless enabled.

Felix Schneider: For allowlist, my vote: Finance + Console Eng + Telemetry Eng + SRE oncall (so they can debug alerts).

Marcus Lin: +1 on group-based only. Also: what’s the plan for auditability? We said access should be attributable (who viewed / queried).

Chloe Martin: PR adds structured audit log on dashboard query endpoints (user_id, query shape, filters). Not logging raw results.

Marcus Lin: Good. Please make sure we’re not writing customer identifiers into logs if someone filters by customer_id (even if we don’t expose it in UI).

Jada Williams: Do we even allow `customer_id` filter? Thought v1 was strictly model/region/tier.

Felix Schneider: In contract sketch we left a placeholder param for internal debugging, but UI doesn’t surface it. In this PR it’s blocked at API layer unless `console:unit_econ:debug` which we are NOT granting.

Marcus Lin: Perfect. Keep it that way.

Dylan Brooks: On allowlist: start conservative. I’d like:
- Chloe, Felix, Nora (Console)
- Logan, Nadia, Mei, Caleb (Telemetry/Analytics)
- Sean/Allison (SRE/Obs)
- Laura + Rishi (Finance)
- Marissa + Aditya (Product)
Then expand once we’ve got finance signoff.

Laura Bennett: +1 conservative. Also please exclude broad groups like “all-engineering”. This is sensitive and the metric may move with rate card updates.

Rishi Malhotra: Agree. I want the initial group to be only folks directly involved in methodology + build. Once reconciled, can expand to exec staff.

Logan Wright: Operationally, please add oncall group(s) as well (eng-sre oncall). When an alert fires, the oncall needs to get into the dashboard to verify completeness vs UI.

Sean Gallagher: +1. But prefer a separate group `unit-econ-dashboard-oncall` so we can rotate membership without touching the main allowlist.

Allison Grant: That would also make it easier to keep least-privilege. Oncall group gets read-only.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Implementation detail: if we split groups, we should map both groups to the same RBAC permission `console:unit_econ:read` and keep the feature flag check the same.

Chloe Martin: Makes sense. I can update PR to accept either group (internal + oncall) and grant same permission.

Nora Ahmed: Small UX note: if user hits the URL without permission, we show “Permission denied”. If user doesn’t have feature flag, we 404. Is that intended?

Marcus Lin: Prefer 404 when flag is off (reduces discoverability), and permission denied when flag is on but no access.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: That’s what we do in other internal tooling pages too.

Dylan Brooks: Cool. What should we name the groups? Please align with Okta naming conventions (Marcus can sanity check).

Marcus Lin: Suggested:
- `okta:console-unit-econ-dashboard-internal`
- `okta:console-unit-econ-dashboard-oncall`
And RBAC role names should not include “okta:” prefix, just map them.

Chloe Martin: PR currently uses `unit-econ-dashboard-internal` (legacy). I’ll adjust to the Okta-ish names + update the access doc snippet.

Laura Bennett: Who owns adding Finance folks to the group? We can’t do it ourselves in Okta.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I can file an IT request once we have the final list. For now, for staging, we can add via the internal authz admin UI (but prod needs Okta group).

Rishi Malhotra: Initial list from Finance: me + Laura only for v1. If we need a backup, maybe add Beth (FinOps) but she’s not in the project doc.

Dylan Brooks: Let’s stick to the named stakeholders for now. We can expand later.

Logan Wright: For telemetry: add Nadia + Mei + Caleb + Cole. (Cole needs read access to validate rate card joins.)

Chloe Martin: Noted.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: One more: make sure the flag is environment-scoped. We don’t want it accidentally on in prod from a dev toggle.

Felix Schneider: It’s stored in LaunchDarkly with separate envs. Default off everywhere. PR adds it to “internal” project only.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Great.

Marcus Lin: Can you drop the final policy snippet here so I can sanity check the shape?

Chloe Martin: Sure, current policy block (will update group names):
```yaml
permissions:
  - name: console:unit_econ:read
    resources:
      - console_page:unit_economics_dashboard
conditions:
  - feature_flag: console.unit_econ_dashboard_internal
bindings:
  - subjects:
      - group: unit-econ-dashboard-internal
    permissions:
      - console:unit_econ:read
```

Marcus Lin: Looks fine. With two groups you’ll have two bindings. Also ensure `conditions` are evaluated per-request not just at login.

Felix Schneider: It’s per-request.

Sean Gallagher: Re oncall group: we need this in place before ENG-8429 alerts go live, otherwise oncall is blind.

Chloe Martin: Yep. I’ll push an update today with:
- split groups (internal + oncall)
- rename groups to Okta convention
- keep read-only
Then request reviews again.

Dylan Brooks: Thanks. Once updated, I’ll approve.

Laura Bennett: Same. Please ping when merged so we can schedule the Finance dogfood.

Chloe Martin: Will do.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: :thumbsup: ping me for the Okta/IT request once you have the exact roster.

Marcus Lin: :white_check_mark: I’ll re-review after the group rename change.

title: #eng: 1738266018
preview:
Kira Thompson: Dropping the draft Confluence runbook here for review before we socialize w/ Support + SE. Please be brutal 🙏 https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/PRIV/Control+Plane+Backup+Runbook+(Draft)

Elliot Price: Skimmed. Overall good structure, but there are a few spots that will confuse customers.
- Step 0 “Prereqs” is too light. We need explicit versions + permissions.
- The “quiesce” section reads like it’s optional, but later we assume we did it.
- Validation section is mostly “check pods are running” — we need functional checks.

Sean Gallagher: +1. From SRE view, I can’t tell what a successful backup *looks like*.
Runbook says “verify artifact integrity” but doesn’t show an actual command/output or what to do if it fails.
Also missing paging/alerting tie-in (even if just “see dashboard X”).

Kira Thompson: Noted. Can you call out the exact missing prereqs you want listed?

Sean Gallagher: Minimum:
- `kubectl` access w/ cluster-admin OR specific RBAC (and list the verbs/resources)
- storage backend configured (S3/NFS) and tested write/read
- KMS configured (if using envelope) and “kms outage behavior” statement
- time sync check (we hit clock skew in staging once)
- “do you need Velero installed?” (we say “optional” but then reference it later)

Ethan Park: On the quiesce bit: for control plane we *do* need to pause reconcilers during snapshot or you can get partially applied state. Draft currently says “if needed” which is too vague.
We should specify:
1) scale down `redwood-controller-manager` (or set `--reconcile=false` if we support that)
2) confirm no migrations running
3) then snapshot DB
4) then resume

Benji Okafor: Agree. Also the runbook says “snapshot config DB” but doesn’t name it. For Private we’ve got `rw-config-postgres` (statefulset) in most installs, but some customers run external Postgres.
We should split into “managed in-cluster DB” vs “external DB” branches and put a giant warning that we don’t handle external DB snapshotting beyond “use your DB vendor snapshot”.

Kira Thompson: Good point. I can add a decision tree.

Hanae Suzuki: Security gaps:
- We say “encrypted at rest” but don’t say how the DEK is generated / where KEK lives (esp for customer-managed keys).
- Need explicit statement: backups must NOT contain plaintext secret material. We only store *references* / metadata, and the restore expects secrets to be provisioned via K8s secret store / external KMS.
- If we *do* include any K8s Secrets for bootstrap (I saw a line implying that), we should be super explicit and likely remove it.

Elliot Price: +1. The draft currently says “export secrets” in one step (under “collect k8s resources”). That’ll set off alarms.
Maybe reword as “export secret references + verify secrets exist in target cluster”.

Bruno Silva: Also the “collect k8s resources” section is confusing. Are we backing up:
A) Redwood control plane state only (DB + object store manifests)
B) full cluster resources (Velero)
Right now it’s kind of both.
My suggestion: keep the primary path as “Redwood backup artifact” via installer CLI, and make Velero an appendix for cluster-level stuff.

Talia Benson: From program POV: we should align with the ADR language. If the decision is “manifest + encrypted tarball w/ versioned schema”, the runbook should mirror those terms.
If there’s a dependency on PR-512 compatibility checks, call it out.

Connor O'Brien: Release/packaging nit: the runbook uses `latest` in a couple examples (`curl .../installer/latest`). For on-prem / regulated, we must pin versions.
Also add a note: use the *same* installer version for restore as used for backup (or newer only if compatible). And point to compatibility matrix once it exists.

Kira Thompson: Re “successful backup looks like” — do we want to include sample `backup create` output?

Benji Okafor: Yes. Something like:
- command: `rw-private backup create --output s3://... --kms-key ...`
- expected: “manifest.json written”, “artifact.enc size > 0”, “sha256 verified”, “backup_id=...”, “format_version=v1”
- then a “where to find logs” section.

Sean Gallagher: And for restore, include an explicit “abort criteria”. Example:
- if post-restore health checks fail for >15 min
- if migrations auto-start unexpectedly
- if routing policies differ from manifest
Right now it’s “rollback if needed” which is too hand-wavy.

Ethan Park: Validation gaps after restore:
We need at least:
- hit `/readyz` and `/healthz` for control-plane services (or whatever endpoints we have)
- run “tenant list” / “models list” from the control plane API and compare counts
- verify routing policy objects restored (the ones that pin model versions)
- generate a small inference request end-to-end (if data plane is up)

Markus Klein: Adding SE/customer POV: the draft assumes they know what “tenant config” includes. We should spell out examples:
- org/tenant IDs
- API keys? (probably not backed up)
- model pins / routing rules
- quotas
Also a FAQ: “Will my audit logs be restored?” (answer probably “export separately”).

Hanae Suzuki: Re API keys: please don’t imply we back up customer secrets. If keys are stored in DB, we need a clear story. If not, then runbook should say “re-provision credentials post-restore”.

Elliot Price: Another confusing thing: prereqs don’t mention CRDs. On restore, if CRDs aren’t installed yet, applying manifests fails (we saw that in SUP-18477).
Add a prerequisite: “install same Helm chart version / CRDs first, then restore state”.

Bruno Silva: +1. Maybe explicitly list bootstrap order:
1) install CRDs
2) install base chart (but keep reconcilers paused)
3) restore DB/object state
4) resume reconcilers
5) run validation

Sean Gallagher: Also: “where artifacts are stored” section should include retention + immutability guidance.
For S3: recommend bucket versioning + Object Lock if they want ransomware posture.
For NFS: at least mention snapshotting / read-only export pattern.

Connor O'Brien: Small doc clarity: use consistent names “backup artifact”, “backup bundle”, “snapshot”. Currently switches terms and it’s easy to misread.

Kira Thompson: This is super helpful. Action items I’m taking:
- Expand Prereqs (RBAC, storage, KMS, time sync, CRDs, pinned versions)
- Make quiesce mandatory + concrete steps
- Split in-cluster vs external DB
- Clarify scope: primary = Redwood artifact; Velero appendix
- Add sample CLI output + “what success looks like”
- Add explicit restore bootstrap order + abort criteria
- Beef up validation with functional/API checks + routing policy verification

Ethan Park: I’ll send you exact component names + the “pause reconcilers” approach we want to standardize on. We should avoid telling people to `kubectl scale` random deployments if we can provide a supported flag.

Sean Gallagher: If you add a “validation checklist” section, I can propose the minimal smoke tests we want on-call to run. We’ll want it to match what the automated restore drill runs.

Kira Thompson: Perfect. I’ll push v2 today and re-drop for final pass.

title: #eng: 1738016620
preview:
Dylan Brooks: quick perf review on the usage insights breakdown queries. seeing p95 ~6-9s for 7d on a mid-size workspace in staging. 30d is basically unusable (timeouts). we need ideas before we lock the API contract.

Chloe Martin: yeah, current query path is doing: route_normalization view -> join metering_events (raw) -> group by workspace_id, project_id, route, model, day. It’s scanning way too much.

Felix Schneider: pulled a trace from the query service. breakdown endpoint is hitting warehouse with ~3.2TB scanned for 30d (🤢). Also we’re doing `COUNT(DISTINCT request_id)` and joining retries/fallback events from the same raw table.

Logan Wright: not surprising. raw metering_events is partitioned by event_ts but we’re not getting good partition pruning b/c the query wraps timestamp filters in some date_trunc + timezone conversion.

Mei Lin: +1. In the query I saw `WHERE date_trunc('day', convert_timezone('UTC', :tz, event_ts)) BETWEEN ...` which forces compute on every row.

Dylan Brooks: yep. That’s from the API supporting timezone-based day boundaries. We can move TZ boundary logic to the app and pass UTC bounds?

Nadia Rahman: careful: finance wants day buckets aligned w/ workspace timezone for exports. but we can still pass UTC start/end and do the trunc in UTC if we precompute a “workspace_local_day” at ingest/agg time.

Thomas Baird: perf suggestion: don’t query raw for console. we should ship an aggregated fact table keyed by (workspace_id, project_id, hour_bucket/day_bucket, route_template, model_family, model_version_bucket, deployment_mode, region). Then the console query becomes a small scan + simple group/rollup.

Chloe Martin: we’re already implementing the aggregation job (ENG-3812). but current staging is still querying raw because the agg table is missing token breakdown + cache stats.

Mei Lin: we can extend the agg schema to include the expensive stuff so the breakdown query is a single table read:
- tokens_in, tokens_out
- cache_hit_tokens, cache_miss_tokens (or hit_rate numerator/denominator)
- retry_count, fallback_count
- streaming_request_count (or streaming_tokens_out)
- latency_ms_sum + latency_ms_p50/p95? (latency percentiles are tricky)

Allison Grant: for latency, we probably don’t want to compute percentiles ad hoc for every filter. if we need p95 in-console, either:
1) maintain t-digest sketches per key per hour/day (warehouse-specific), or
2) precompute fixed buckets (e.g., <250ms, 250-500, 500-1s, 1-2s, 2-5s, >5s) and derive “latency-correlated spend” from those.

Felix Schneider: right now the query is doing a join against `request_latency_events` anyway. it’s another full scan.

Logan Wright: materialized view option: create MV `usage_insights_route_model_hourly` from metering_events w/ the dims we care about + route normalized at write time. then query service just filters + aggregates to requested time grain.

Thomas Baird: +1, MV or scheduled job. if we go w/ job, do hourly partitions + cluster by workspace_id, project_id, route_template. that should keep scans bounded.

Dylan Brooks: do we have numbers on cardinality? route_template might blow up if normalization misses.

Chloe Martin: in staging data, top offenders are OpenAI-compat endpoints where path params aren’t templated consistently. That’s in ENG-3813. once we canonicalize, route cardinality is manageable.

Nadia Rahman: also model aliases are causing duplicates right now. if we aggregate on raw model_id, we’ll still have weirdness. we should aggregate on (model_family, model_version_bucket) and keep model_id for drilldown only.

Mei Lin: +1. plus, we can keep a separate “model_id -> bucket” mapping table and update it, but aggregation should store the bucketed dims for stable reporting.

Felix Schneider: tactical fix for this week: change the breakdown query to hit the agg table for tokens + cost, and only join raw for the “what changed” explanations if needed.

Allison Grant: i’d rather we don’t have the console path ever touching raw for beta. observability-wise it’s hard to SLO if query shape changes per filter.

Logan Wright: another easy win: restrict time ranges for interactive queries. e.g. hard cap 14d for breakdown view unless user requests export (async). export can run heavier queries w/ longer timeout.

Dylan Brooks: product will push back on caps but we can justify with “interactive perf.” export endpoint can do 30d+.

Chloe Martin: also pagination. we’re currently doing `ORDER BY cost DESC LIMIT 500` but without pre-aggregation it still computes global group-by first. With pre-agg it becomes cheap.

Thomas Baird: indexes / clustering keys matter: if warehouse supports it, cluster on (workspace_id, day_bucket) then secondary on (route_template, model_bucket). most queries are constrained by workspace + time.

Mei Lin: and don’t compute “totals” as a separate query. we can add a rollup row in the agg table or compute it from same dataset (less round trips).

Felix Schneider: i’ll paste the worst offender query plan summary:
```
Scan metering_events (partitioned by event_ts): 29,184 partitions
Filter: computed expression on event_ts prevents pruning
Join route_normalized_view: broadcast
Group by: workspace_id, project_id, route, model
Distinct: request_id
Spill: yes (disk)
Total time: 48.2s (timed out at 60s)
```

Dylan Brooks: ok. action items?

Logan Wright: propose:
1) adjust API to pass UTC bounds, no tz conversion inside WHERE
2) prioritize MV/job for `usage_insights_route_model_hourly` including tokens/cache/retries/fallback/streaming counts
3) add clustering/partition scheme + backfill last 30d for beta accounts only
4) for latency: choose histogram buckets (fast) now; consider t-digest later

Allison Grant: add alerting around agg lag + query p95. if we’re doing MVs, we need freshness indicators in console anyway.

Chloe Martin: i’ll take (1) + coordinate with Felix on query service changes. Logan/Mei can you spec the MV/job columns today so we can update ENG-3812?

Mei Lin: yep, I’ll post a proposed schema in the ENG-3812 ticket comments.

Thomas Baird: i can run a quick benchmark once schema is set. we should test 7d/30d scans on a “big” workspace and confirm p95 < 1.5s for interactive.

Dylan Brooks: 👍 let’s do it. also: for beta, we can message “30d+ recommended via export.” will sync w/ Marissa/Paula.

Nadia Rahman: one more note: reconciliation vs billing—ensure aggregation uses the same rounding policy as billing hooks (or stores unrounded units and only rounds at display/export). don’t let perf changes break correctness.

Chloe Martin: good call. we’ll store raw microdollars/token_units in agg and format at the edge.

Felix Schneider: I’ll open a PR to remove timezone transforms from WHERE and to enforce a max time range for interactive endpoints (configurable) until MV is live.

Allison Grant: please also log `warehouse_bytes_scanned` and `warehouse_query_time_ms` per request in the query service. makes perf regressions obvious.

Dylan Brooks: done. closing loop once we have new benchmark numbers.

title: #eng: 1737581002
preview:
Amina Yusuf: Working on private-installer audit events (upgrade/rollback/config apply). Main open question: how do we represent operator identity in Private when someone runs `redwood-private install/upgrade` from a bastion / laptop? We can’t always rely on OIDC.

Diego Santos: yeah this is the parity gap vs Hosted/Dedicated. Hosted we have `actor.user_id` + auth context + source_ip. In Private, “operator” might be (a) human running CLI, (b) automation (Ansible, Argo, etc). We should model both.

Vivek Kulkarni: +1. For enterprise customers, they keep asking “who did the upgrade” and “who changed exporter settings”. Even if it’s “unknown automation” we need something consistent.

Kevin Osei: Reminder from schema discussions: we need stable semantics, minimal PII, and good dedupe. For Private installer events, propose:
- `actor.type`: `human` | `service` | `unknown`
- `actor.id`: if known (OIDC sub, customer SSO GUID, or service principal)
- `actor.display_name`: optional, but would be high-cardinality + often PII. We should avoid unless we’re hashing.
- `actor.email_sha256`: optional if we have email, but only if customer opts in? (also doesn’t help if no email)

Hanae Suzuki: +1 avoid raw email. We already have redaction rules draft that treats email as PII. Hash is OK but still sensitive-ish; needs consistent salt story (can’t be Redwood secret in air-gapped env).

Amina Yusuf: Here’s what the installer currently can reliably collect:
- CLI has `--operator-id` (we can add) + `--operator-type` (human/service)
- In interactive mode, we can optionally prompt `operator` (freeform) but that’s the worst for cardinality
- In k8s, we can read `KUBECONFIG` context user name (but that’s messy / sometimes includes email)
- We always have `installer_instance_id` and `installer_version`

Rafael Mendes: From incident forensics POV, even “unknown” is better than nothing as long as we have correlation IDs + the host it ran on. Can we capture `execution_environment` like `host.hostname` or `node`? (but careful: could leak internal naming)

Sean Gallagher: SRE take: do NOT block upgrade/rollback on identity collection. During an outage we need to run rollback fast. Identity should be best-effort.

Diego Santos: Agree. We can treat actor fields as best-effort but the event itself must be emitted. We should add `actor.attribution`: `explicit` (flag provided) vs `inferred` (kube context) vs `unknown`.

Kevin Osei: That helps for customer guidance too: “If you want strong operator attribution, pass operator-id via automation or configure OIDC.”

Connor O'Brien: Release perspective: we should avoid adding an interactive prompt by default. Flags/env vars are safer for automation.

Amina Yusuf: Proposal v1 (please poke holes):
- Add flags/env:
  - `--actor-type` / `RW_AUDIT_ACTOR_TYPE` in {human, service}
  - `--actor-id` / `RW_AUDIT_ACTOR_ID` (string, recommended stable GUID)
  - `--actor-idp` / `RW_AUDIT_ACTOR_IDP` (optional, e.g. okta, aad, local)
- Installer emits audit events with:
  - `actor.type` from flag else `unknown`
  - `actor.id` from flag else empty
  - `actor.attribution` = explicit|unknown
  - `client.name` = `redwood-private-installer`, `client.version` = installer version
  - `source.ip` omitted (or null) in Private
- Add `operator` info as a namespaced extension field if we need more later (e.g. `private.operator.*`)

Hanae Suzuki: looks good. One tweak: if we include `actor.idp`, limit allowed values (enum) to avoid random strings -> cardinality.

Amina Yusuf: Working on private-installer audit events (upgrade/rollback/config apply). Main open question: how do we represent operator identity in Private when someone runs `redwood-private install/upgrade` from a bastion / laptop? We can’t always rely on OIDC.

Diego Santos: yeah this is the parity gap vs Hosted/Dedicated. Hosted we have `actor.user_id` + auth context + source_ip. In Private, “operator” might be (a) human running CLI, (b) automation (Ansible, Argo, etc). We should model both.

Vivek Kulkarni: +1. For enterprise customers, they keep asking “who did the upgrade” and “who changed exporter settings”. Even if it’s “unknown automation” we need something consistent.

Kevin Osei: Reminder from schema discussions: we need stable semantics, minimal PII, and good dedupe. For Private installer events, propose:
- `actor.type`: `human` | `service` | `unknown`
- `actor.id`: if known (OIDC sub, customer SSO GUID, or service principal)
- `actor.display_name`: optional, but would be high-cardinality + often PII. We should avoid unless we’re hashing.
- `actor.email_sha256`: optional if we have email, but only if customer opts in? (also doesn’t help if no email)

Hanae Suzuki: +1 avoid raw email. We already have redaction rules draft that treats email as PII. Hash is OK but still sensitive-ish; needs consistent salt story (can’t be Redwood secret in air-gapped env).

Amina Yusuf: Here’s what the installer currently can reliably collect:
- CLI has `--operator-id` (we can add) + `--operator-type` (human/service)
- In interactive mode, we can optionally prompt `operator` (freeform) but that’s the worst for cardinality
- In k8s, we can read `KUBECONFIG` context user name (but that’s messy / sometimes includes email)
- We always have `installer_instance_id` and `installer_version`

Rafael Mendes: From incident forensics POV, even “unknown” is better than nothing as long as we have correlation IDs + the host it ran on. Can we capture `execution_environment` like `host.hostname` or `node`? (but careful: could leak internal naming)

Sean Gallagher: SRE take: do NOT block upgrade/rollback on identity collection. During an outage we need to run rollback fast. Identity should be best-effort.

Diego Santos: Agree. We can treat actor fields as best-effort but the event itself must be emitted. We should add `actor.attribution`: `explicit` (flag provided) vs `inferred` (kube context) vs `unknown`.

Kevin Osei: That helps for customer guidance too: “If you want strong operator attribution, pass operator-id via automation or configure OIDC.”

Connor O'Brien: Release perspective: we should avoid adding an interactive prompt by default. Flags/env vars are safer for automation.

- Add flags/env:
  - `--actor-type` / `RW_AUDIT_ACTOR_TYPE` in {human, service}

  - `actor.type` from flag else `unknown`
  - `actor.id` from flag else empty
  - `actor.attribution` = explicit|unknown
  - `client.name` = `redwood-private-installer`, `client.version` = installer version
